## Why we penalize a linear model

Penalized regression keeps the linear-model workflow but changes the fitting rule.
Instead of only minimizing prediction error on the training data, we also penalize
large coefficients. That makes the model less eager to chase noise, especially when
predictors are numerous, correlated, or only weakly informative.

In `TEM 0052`, this concept matters because regularization is usually the first
credible upgrade after the plain linear baseline. It is still interpretable, still
fast, and often much more stable out of sample.

## Ridge, lasso, and elastic net

All three methods start from the linear-regression loss and add a penalty term:

$$
\hat{\beta}_{\text{ridge}}
= \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^\top \beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_{\text{lasso}}
= \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^\top \beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}|\beta_j|
$$

$$
\hat{\beta}_{\text{elastic net}}
= \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^\top \beta)^2
+ \lambda \left((1-\alpha)\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2 + \alpha \sum_{j=1}^{p}|\beta_j|\right)
$$

The practical differences are the important ones for this course:

- ridge shrinks correlated predictors together and is often a strong predictive default,
- lasso can set some coefficients exactly to zero and gives a rough feature-selection rule,
- elastic net is useful when you want sparsity but also expect correlated predictor groups.

## Regularization is a workflow choice, not a magic fix

Penalization helps only when the rest of the workflow is disciplined.

1. Split or resample the data before comparing models.
2. Standardize predictors inside the modelling pipeline.
3. Tune `lambda` with cross-validation rather than visual guesswork.
4. Compare the penalized model to the plain linear baseline on the same evaluation rule.
5. Check whether the gain is predictive stability, lower error, simpler coefficients, or all three.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students when they would choose ridge over lasso if the main goal is prediction
rather than sparse storytelling.
:::

## What students should notice in the house-prices lab

The house-prices exercise is not there to prove that lasso always wins. It is there
to make the trade-offs visible:

- ridge often improves stability when many features move together,
- lasso can produce a smaller and easier-to-report model,
- elastic net is worth considering when pure lasso is too unstable,
- and the best-looking training fit is not the same thing as the best out-of-sample workflow.

That is why the lab asks for the same split, the same metrics, and a short written
comparison of the modelling choices.

## What to report

When students present a penalized regression workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should
usually report:

- how predictors were scaled and preprocessed,
- which tuning rule selected `lambda` or `l1_ratio`,
- the baseline linear-model result used for comparison,
- the out-of-sample metrics that justified the final choice,
- and one short explanation of whether the penalty improved prediction, stability,
  interpretability, or only complexity.
